preamble: The phonetic transformation from a voiced labio-velar approximant /w/ to a bilabial nasal /m/ is a documented historical sound change, often occurring in environments influenced by nasalization or as a result of the loss of the approximant quality.
phoneticEffects: This shift involves a change in the manner of articulation from an approximant to a nasal, accompanied by a slight adjustment in the place of articulation from labio-velar to bilabial

examples.note: Universal conversion of *w to [m] before a nasal vowel.
Proto-Iroquoian *awẽʔ 'water' became Cherokee amaa /àma̋ː/ 'water' when *w was followed by a nasalized vowel.
Proto-Iroquoian *awẽʔ 'water' became Laurentian Ame 'id.' when *w was followed by a nasalized vowel.
Wyandot awę' /aˈwɛ̃ʔ/ 'water' pronounced [aˈmɛ̃ʔ] when *w was followed by a nasalized vowel.
Intervocalic /w/ changed to /m/ as part of the loss of phonemic /w/.
Nasalization of /w/ to /m/ in words like maŋa.

sources: MANDAEANS v. MANDAIC LANGUAGE - Encyclopaedia Iranica. (2009, July 20).
Nasal harmony in Hoocąk and Mandan*.
How were labial consonants substituted in loanwords into Iroquoian? (2025, July 7).
How far west into Asia have Eskimo languages been spoken, and which ones? – Études/Inuit/Studies - Érudit.
